operating_state: Net sales increased 6.5% to $43.8 billion in Q2 2022 driven by 5.8% comparable sales growth, reflecting 9.0% higher average ticket offset by 3.1% fewer customer transactions. All merchandising departments posted positive comparable sales with Building Materials, Plumbing, Millwork, Paint, and Hardware exceeding company average. Online sales grew 12.0% and represented 13.9% of total sales as customers continued leveraging digital platforms.
cost_structure: Gross margin compressed to 33.1% from 33.2% primarily due to supply chain network investments and higher product and transportation costs, partially offset by retail price increases. SG&A improved to 15.2% of sales from 15.6% driven by positive comparable sales leverage and expense management, partially offset by hourly associate wage investments and operational efficiency investments.
material_events: Board announced 15% quarterly dividend increase to $1.90 per share in February 2022. Craig Menear retired as Chairman effective September 30, 2022, with CEO Edward Decker elected as new Chairman effective October 1, 2022. Company completed $3.0 billion debt issuance in September 2022 across three tranches with maturities from 2025 to 2052.
macro_exposures: Stronger U.S. dollar negatively impacted net sales by $129 million in Q2 2022. Company faces ongoing global supply chain disruption requiring strategic inventory investments and merchandise pull-forward for second half events. Higher inflation drove increased product and transportation costs while supporting average ticket growth through retail price increases.
forward_outlook: Management plans approximately $3 billion in capital expenditures for fiscal 2022, targeting roughly 2% of net sales annually. Company maintains disciplined capital allocation prioritizing business investment, dividend payments, then excess cash return through share repurchases. Board approved new $15 billion share repurchase authorization in August 2022 replacing prior authorization.
uncertainty_profile: Inventory turnover declined to 4.5 times from 5.7 times due to strategic inventory investments for higher in-stock levels and supply chain disruption response. Company expanded commercial paper program and backup credit facilities to $5.0 billion each to enhance financial flexibility. No material changes to critical accounting policies or risk factors during the period.
